content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 9, 1990

Leslie M. Liscom, Member
California Horse Racing Board
1010 Hurley Way, Suite 190
Sacramento, CA 95825

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-126

Dear Commissioner Liscom:

This is in response to your request for informal assistance
concerning your duties under the conflict of interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your letter does
not concern a specific governmental decision, we treat your
request as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c)(4)(D).²

QUESTION

Does your employment for an insurance broker whose clients
includes persons or organizations subject to the jurisdiction of
the California Horse Racing Board (the "racing board") preclude
you from participating in racing board decisions affecting these
clients?

CONCLUSION

Whether or not you can participate in decisions affecting the
insurance broker's clients depends on each particular decision's
financial effect on the broker.

_______________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-126
Page 2

FACTS

You are a member of the racing board. You also are employed
as a Senior Vice President for Oland International Insurance
Brokers (the "brokerage firm").

The brokerage firm is based in Los Angeles and has branch
offices in New York and Kansas City. It employs approximately 70
people and has annual revenues of approximately $10 million. The
brokerage firm's clientele consists solely of corporations and
organizations. It has no individuals as clients. The firm's
operations are divided into two main sections: (1) Property and
Casualty Insurance (such as workers' compensation, public
liability, automobile and fire), and (2) Employee Benefits (such
as group medical, life, travel accident, long term disability and
pension plans).

As Senior Vice President of the brokerage firm, your day-to-
day activities are confined to servicing property and casualty
insurances for eight corporate clients, none of whom are regulated
by the racing board. You are involved in neither the solicitation
of new business nor employee benefit coverages. You are paid a
salary, own 1% of the firm's stock, but do not participate in the
firm's profit sharing programs.

The brokerage firm has three clients who are subject to the
racing board's regulation. One is a racing association which pays
approximately $1900 annually to the firm for a long term
disability insurance policy. Another is a racing association
which pays approximately $10,500 annually to the firm for a group
medical and dental insurance policy. The third is a union of
horse racing employees which, through a trust, pays the firm
$45,000 annually for a group medical, life and dental insurance
policy. You have no involvement with any of these organizations
in your work for the brokerage firm.

You also indicate that, in the future, the brokerage firm
could provide insurance services for the company of an individual
who may be regulated by the racing board in a capacity unrelated
to his company (he may, for instance, own a horse). In such a
case, you ask us to assume that the insurance policy will not be
serviced by you and is unrelated to the individual's activities
that are regulated by the racing board.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits each public official from making,
participating in making, or attempting to use his official
position to influence governmental decisions in which he has a

Our File No. I-90-126
Page 3

financial interest. As a member of the racing board, you are a
public official and subject to this requirement.³

Section 87103 states that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on the official or
on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Because you own 1% of the stock in⁴, receive a salary from
and are an employee of the brokerage firm, you have a financial
interest in the firm under Section 87103. Therefore, you are
precluded from participating in decisions of the racing board if
they have a material financial effect on the brokerage firm.

Under the facts presented in your letter, any decisions you
make as a racing board member will have, at the most, an indirect
effect on the brokerage firm. This is because the firm itself is
not regulated in any way by the racing board. Rather, the effect
on the firm, if any, will be indirect in that the decision may
affect firm clients which in turn may affect the insurance
premiums paid by the clients to the firm.




³ Section 82048 defines public official, in pertinent part, as
"every member . . . of a state or local government agency . . ."
Section 82049 defines state agency as "every state office,
division, bureau, board and commission, and the legislature."
(Emphasis added.)

⁴ We presume that 1% of the brokerage firm's stock equals or
exceeds $1,000 in value.

Our File No. I-90-126
Page 4

     It is possible that particular decisions by the racing board
will have an indirect financial effect on the brokerage firm.  For
example, a particular racing board decision may foreseeably affect
a racing association in such a way that it loses members and
thereby cancels its insurance coverage with the brokerage firm.
In such a case, where the indirect financial effects of the
decision on the firm are reasonably foreseeable⁵,
Regulation 18702.2 will apply to determine whether the financial
effects are material.

     Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) sets forth the criteria
necessary to determine whether a governmental decision has a
material financial effect on a business entity.  This regulation
judges material financial effect on the basis of the decision's
effect on a firm's gross revenues, expenses or assets and
according to the market on which the firm's stocks are traded.
For example, if the brokerage firm's stock is traded on the New
York Stock Exchange, a racing board decision would have to affect
the firm's annual gross revenues, assets or liabilities by
$250,000 or more (or by $1 million or more if the firm is a
Fortune 500 company), or affect the firm's expenses by $100,000 or
more (or by $250,000 or more if the firm is a Fortune 500
Company), in order for there to be a material financial effect by
the decision on the firm.  (Regulation 18702.2(a).)  If the firm's
stock is not traded on the New York Stock Exchange subdivisions
(b) through (g) of the regulation will apply, based on the
financial size of the brokerage firm.

     You have not provided information indicating the market on
which the brokerage firm's stock is traded.  You should not
presented us with a particular decision and its possible effect on
the brokerage firm's clients.  Reference to Regulation 18702.2
will assist you in determining the minimum dollar amounts that
apply to the brokerage firm.  Thereafter, you will have to analyze
each particular racing board decision and determine its
foreseeable financial effect on the firm's clients (and,
ultimately, the firm) before deciding whether to participate in
that decision.


_____________________________
     ⁵ In determining whether the effect of a decision is reasonably
foreseeable, certainty is not required, rather there must be a
substantial likelihood that the effect will occur.  If the effect
is no more than a mere possibility it is not reasonably
foreseeable.  (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198, copy
enclosed.)

Our File No. I-90-126
Page 5

I hope that this analysis has been of assistance. Should you
have further questions, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Scott Hallabrin
Counsel, Legal Division

KED:SH:ld

Enclosures
